description: DTE Energy is a Detroit-based utility holding company whose regulated electric and natural gas subsidiaries serve millions of customers across Michigan while pursuing grid, generation, and gas-system modernization under close regulatory oversight.

overview.title: Business profile
paragraphs: DTE Energy operates primarily through two regulated utility products: DTE Electric and DTE Gas. DTE Electric serves roughly 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan, while DTE Gas serves roughly 1.4 million customers across Michigan.

The company also reports non-utility energy businesses, including custom energy solutions, renewable generation, and energy marketing and trading, but the registry entry should start with the regulated utility footprint because that is where DTE's customer dependency is clearest.

overview.title: Products and market position
paragraphs: DTE Electric is the core electricity product: a regulated grid, generation, billing, reliability, and customer-service system bundled into one unavoidable local utility relationship. It is not a software platform so much as a monopoly-shaped public necessity with a website attached.

DTE Gas is the natural gas counterpart, focused on gas distribution, pipeline infrastructure, and decarbonization claims. Both products should be evaluated through reliability, affordability, customer lock-in, public accountability, and the availability of distributed or community-controlled alternatives.

overview.title: Transition strategy
paragraphs: DTE frames its current strategy around grid reliability, renewable generation, storage, coal retirement, and net-zero goals for electric and gas operations. Its CleanVision materials describe planned coal exit by 2032, expanded wind and solar, battery storage, and continued reliance on natural gas and nuclear generation during the transition.

For registry purposes, this makes DTE a useful utilities case study: the company is simultaneously a public-service infrastructure operator, a rate-regulated capital spender, and a climate-transition narrator. Those roles do not always point in the same direction, which is where the research should keep its eyebrow raised.

overview.title: Alternative landscape
paragraphs: Potential open, free, or decentralized comparison points include public power models, municipal utilities, rural electric cooperatives, community solar, distributed solar-plus-storage, demand-response aggregators, open energy data standards, home energy management tools, and weatherization or efficiency programs.

Because no technology waves are mapped yet, the first structured sync should avoid over-classifying DTE as a software or clean-tech company. The better initial lens is utility dependence versus customer agency: where households and businesses can choose, generate, conserve, automate, or govern energy differently.
